title: Knotted long stitch
subtitle: A decorative half-hitch knot on each spine stitch, head to tail
excerpt: The knotted long stitch finishes each spine stitch with a half-hitch knot on the outside of the spine, creating a bead-like row of knots from head to tail. It is structurally the same as a plain long stitch but slower: the knot must be pulled snug before the next station. The resulting spine is a feature of the book, not just a structural seam.
sourceNotes: Long-stitch binding traditions pre-date printed books; examples survive in Ethiopian and Coptic binding. The long-stitch structure is documented in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), Internet Archive. The knotted variation and its structural distinction from the plain long stitch are synthesised from observed bookbinding practice; Keith Smith, Non-Adhesive Binding (1990) describes chain-link and knotted variation structures but is in copyright: structural description only.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding stitch where the thread exits the signature at each sewing station, passes along the outside of the spine to the next station on the same signature, and re-enters. Each stitch is visible on the outside of the spine as a horizontal span of thread. Multiple signatures are sewn one on top of another to build the text block.

A simple locking knot made by looping the working thread around the standing thread and pulling through. In the knotted long stitch, a half-hitch is worked on the outside of the spine each time the thread exits a sewing station, pulling the knot snug before continuing to the next station.

A group of sheets folded together to form one section of a book. In a long-stitch binding, each signature is sewn independently through its fold; successive signatures are stacked and the thread passes from one to the next.

A pierced hole in a signature through which the needle passes during sewing. For the knotted long stitch, station spacing determines the beat of the knot row: closer stations give a denser, more textured spine.

content.text: The
long stitch
is one of the oldest sewing structures for exposed-spine binding: a thread spans the outside of each
signature
's spine between
sewing stations
. The knotted variation finishes each station exit with a
half-hitch knot

on the spine face before the thread continues to the next station. The knots form a bead-like ridge running head to tail on each signature's spine section, and the accumulated rows of knots give the finished spine a textured, visible structure.

items.name: Bookbinding thread, linen (waxed)
items.qty: primary thread: approx. 8× spine length per signature

items.name: Text paper, 90 gsm, grain short, folded into 4 or 5-sheet signatures
Decorative thread in a contrasting colour (linen or hemp)
items.qty: optional: for the knotted stitches only: approx. 3× the spine length total
items.substitutions: Using the same thread throughout gives a more graphic, unified look

content.text: The move
Fold all signatures and press the folds crisp with the bone folder. For five-sheet signatures at 90 gsm, the spine fold is approximately 2 mm thick: a manageable weight for the knotted stitch.

Mark the sewing stations on the spine of one signature. Choose an odd number of stations for a balanced knot pattern: five stations work well for an A5-height book. Station 1: 15 mm from head. Station 5: 15 mm from tail. Stations 2, 3, 4 evenly spaced between. Transfer these marks to all signatures using the first as a template.

Pierce the sewing stations in every signature from inside the fold outward. Hold the signature in a piercing jig or in one hand with the spine facing up. The awl enters at the fold and exits on the spine face.

Thread the needle with waxed linen eight times the spine length. Begin the first signature: enter station 1 from inside, exit on the spine, leaving a 60 mm tail inside. Do not knot at station 1 of the first signature.

Carry the thread along the spine to station 2. Before re-entering, work the half-hitch: loop the thread around itself at the exit point. Pass the needle under the loop so it forms a ring on the spine surface, then pull the ring snug against the spine. The knot should sit flush on the signature fold. Re-enter station 2 from outside, pass through the signature, and exit at station 3 on the spine. Knot at station 3. Continue to station 4 (knot), station 5 (knot). Tie the tail at station 5 with a half-hitch rather than a knot.

Place the second signature on top of the first, spines touching. Sew the second signature in the reverse direction (tail to head), entering each station from outside and working the half-hitch knot on exit before carrying to the next station. When the thread at the head of the second signature has been knotted, take a small stitch under the corresponding thread span on the first signature's spine before entering station 1 of the second signature from outside. This connecting stitch joins the two signatures.

Continue adding signatures, alternating sewing direction and connecting at each end before continuing. The connection at head and tail is a simple catch stitch under the previous signature's end thread, not a kettle stitch: the long stitch does not use a kettle station.
Finish the last signature with two half-hitches around the nearest catch stitch. Trim both tails to 5 mm and tuck inside the signature fold.

items.problem: Knots are different sizes across the spine
items.cause: The half-hitch pull force varies from station to station. A loose pull leaves a larger knot; a tight pull leaves a smaller one.
items.fix: Work each half-hitch with the same wrist motion: loop, insert needle, pull in one direction (always toward the head or always toward the tail) with the same force. Consistency matters more than strength.

items.problem: Knots slide along the spine rather than sitting at the station
items.cause: The thread has been looped but not tightened before the needle re-enters the hole.
items.fix: After forming the half-hitch loop, pull it down to the exit hole before pulling the knot snug. The knot should seat at the hole, not float on the thread span between stations.

items.cause: The wax coating has worn off in a heavily worked section, or the thread has been over-tensioned.
items.fix: Wax the thread again from the current position. If a break has already occurred, join a new length with a reef knot inside the signature fold, trim the tail short, and continue. A reef knot inside the fold is invisible from the spine.

content.text: Where it leads

The knotted long stitch uses the same sewing sequence as the caterpillar stitch. The decorative element differs: caterpillar makes a chain link between signatures; knotted long stitch makes a knot on each signature's span. Both are exposed-spine structures and suit a limp-cover or soft-board book.

A variation: use two different thread weights in contrasting colours, alternating a fine carry thread through the holes and a heavier thread for the half-hitch only. This gives a two-texture spine where the carrying thread is almost invisible and the knots stand out.
